_provenance: SD-LEO-FEAT-AUTHOR-VENTURE-DESIGN-001 — authored build-time design generation inputs (craft do-rules inverted from the Stage-17 audit rubrics in shared-design-prompts.json, which stays byte-identical as the audit SSOT; every craft do_rule carries an anchor quoted verbatim from its prompt's audit text, enforced by the anchor-provenance unit test so the two forms cannot drift silently). Conversion do-rules distilled from UX Peak; motion grammar per chairman direction; exemplar distilled from the chairman-approved MarketLens landing.

2.rule: Write the hero headline + subhead so a first-time visitor can say what this is, who it is for, and the outcome — in under 5 seconds. If the value proposition needs the rest of the page, the hero has failed.
Make the hero headline the single loudest element above the fold; the subhead supports without competing; any eyebrow hugs the headline.
2.anchor: single loudest element above the fold

2.rule: Label every CTA action-led and specific ("Start free", "Get the API key") — never a generic "Submit" or "Learn more" — and repeat the primary CTA label identically every time it appears.
Write section headings that alone tell the page's story; never place a wall of text between the visitor and the CTA.
Set body copy at roughly 45-80 characters per line; allow no orphans, no widows, and no awkward mid-word breaks at viewport edges.
Vary the gaps inside a text block deliberately: an eyebrow hugs its heading, the heading breathes into the body — the gaps should never all be equal.
Meet WCAG AA on every text element (4.5:1 body, 3:1 large type), and prefer a solid color token over opacity tricks wherever a solid token reads more clearly.
Style uppercase eyebrows consistently across every section: tracking at or above 0.14em, weight 600+, size 10-12px.
Use true micro-typography: smart quotes, real em/en dashes, non-breaking spaces before units, one consistent ellipsis style — and never leave punctuation trapped in an accent style.
Write trust copy with metrics that are specific and consistently formatted — and ONLY real ones taken from venture data; omit the element when no real number exists.
Give every button label whitespace-nowrap so it cannot wrap on narrow viewports.
Verify type at 360px, 768px, 1024px, and 1440px: no heading may break unnaturally, overflow, or lose hierarchy at any width.

3.rule: Keep the value-prop headline AND the primary CTA visible without scrolling at both 1440px and 390px — a below-the-fold primary CTA on mobile is a build failure.
Let the hero silhouette set the page: later sections echo the hero's column ratio instead of inventing their own.
Compose a scroll narrative — hero, proof, problem/solution, how-it-works, closing CTA — whose section silhouettes relate, with one clear conversion path rather than competing ones.
Place the primary CTA at the hero and again near the end; never bury it and never dilute it with equal-weight CTAs.
Design social proof as a deliberate, evenly-weighted band of logos or metrics — never an afterthought row.
Earn every centering: center only short, symmetrical, hero-like moments. Never center multi-line body copy — that is defaulted, not designed.
Line up an intro block's edges with the content below it, and keep the left edges of stacked elements (eyebrow, heading, body, CTA) on one vertical line.
Give every asymmetric layout an intentional text-to-image width relationship — half, two-thirds, or golden ratio — never an arbitrary split.
Hold a coherent vertical-rhythm tier (tight / standard / hero) across neighboring sections; never leave one section a full tier off from both neighbors.
3.anchor: full tier off from both neighbors

3.rule: Give every text child inside a flex column an explicit width or items-stretch so it can never collapse to min-content on narrow viewports.
When two consecutive sections share a background token, mark the boundary with a divider, contrast band, or asymmetric padding.
3.anchor: divider, contrast band, or asymmetric padding

3.rule: Hold each section's silhouette at 360px, 768px, 1024px, and 1440px — do not let the composition collapse into a generic single column too early.

4.rule: Treat the hero as the LCP element: nothing above it may block render; serve hero media in a modern format (AVIF/WebP), correctly sized, with fetchpriority set high.
Make the primary CTA interactive immediately — no hydration delay may block the first click.
Give every image explicit width/height or an aspect-ratio so it reserves space; lazy-load below the fold. Layout shift from unreserved media is the number-one build defect.
Load web fonts with font-display swap, preload and subset the primary display face — no invisible-text flash, no late reflow.
Build to the vitals budget: LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms — identify the LCP element and keep everything above it render-clean.
Animate transform/opacity only — never width, height, top, or left; nothing that forces reflow.

Build for real content, not demo copy: a much longer headline, a single unbroken word or URL, a list with 0, 1, or 50 items, and a failed image must all degrade gracefully with space reserved and zero shift.
Design every interactive state: hover, focus-visible, active, disabled, and a pending state for anything async — a visitor must never click into silence.
Give forms inline validation, an accessible per-field error state (never just a red border), a success state, and a primary CTA disabled until valid.
Ship both themes: verify contrast, tonal depth, and image overlays in light AND dark; take every color from theme tokens, never hardcoded hex.

conversion.label: Conversion psychology (UX Peak distill)

do_rules.rule: Pre-select the most common choice (plan, tier, option) so the low-effort path IS the recommended path — the visitor confirms rather than configures.
Never show the user starting at zero: count progress already made ("step 1 of 3 done", a pre-filled bar) — visible momentum toward a goal increases completion.
Give real value BEFORE asking for commitment — a preview, a free insight, a usable artifact — so signing up feels like reciprocation, not a toll booth.
Let the visitor build or customize part of the product before signup (their data, their name, their configuration) — people do not abandon what already feels like theirs.
Frame the decision as preventing a concrete loss (the missed insight, the wasted spend, the drifting metric) — avoiding a loss motivates roughly twice as strongly as an equivalent gain.
Anchor the price or effort against a larger relevant number (the cost of the problem, the price of the alternative) so the ask reads small by comparison.
Anticipate the visitor's objections (Is it secure? Can I cancel? How long is setup?) and answer them proactively right next to the CTA — an unanswered question is an exit.
Place a credibility signal (a real customer, award, rating, or security mark) adjacent to the title or CTA so trust transfers at the exact decision moment.
Show the product in real use in the hero — actual UI, actual output — never make the visitor imagine what it does.

conversion.guardrail: HARD GUARDRAIL — INTEGRITY OVER PERSUASION: NEVER fabricate metrics, testimonials, user counts, ratings, or any social-proof number. Use real venture data, or OMIT the element entirely. A conversion principle applied with a fake number destroys the exact trust it exists to build. When real data is missing, that is a MISS to flag — not a blank to invent.

do_rules.rule: Give each screen the micro-animation vocabulary designed for its brand feel: an entry transition, hover states, a loading animation, and a CTA effect.
Pick ONE easing curve and ONE entrance pattern and reuse them across the entire page — motion coherence beats motion variety.
Add subtle scroll-parallax to hero or instrument elements as depth, not spectacle: small offsets, slow deltas, transform/opacity only.
Animate transform and opacity ONLY — never layout properties (width/height/top/left) that force reflow.
Honor prefers-reduced-motion: disable or reduce ALL motion — parallax included — when the user asks for less.
Make motion deliberate, never decorative: every animation must earn its place by directing attention or confirming an action.

exemplar.label: Style exemplar — the chairman-approved MarketLens landing
exemplar.note: Emulate the CHARACTER of this worked example, not its content. It applied exactly these craft + conversion + motion rules to real corpus content and passed the design-quality rubric with the chairman's approval.
character: One corpus-verbatim hero headline as the single loudest element, a supporting tagline that does not compete, and ONE action-led CTA label repeated verbatim on every page where it appears.
Deliberate section silhouettes — hero, a four-block feature grid, a trusted-by proof band — each earning its width and rhythm rather than defaulting to centered columns.
Proof built ONLY from real venture artifacts: every landing section traces to a corpus source, and any element with no real backing data is OMITTED and flagged rather than invented.

A restrained tokenized palette (one primary, one sparing accent), self-hosted brand fonts actually loaded via @font-face, WCAG-AA hover and focus states everywhere, and a compact vector logo instead of heavy raster assets.
